metadata.title: Why the Bacteria That Cause Ulcers Also Cause Cancer
metadata.color_semantics: TEAL = eradicated / inflammation-free / no cancer progression; CRIMSON = persistent infection / inflammatory damage / advancing cascade
metadata.note: EXCLUSIONS: no CagA biochemistry; no H. pylori virulence factor taxonomy; no HPV or other viral carcinogens in this video; no aflatoxin interaction; no Nobel Prize background. All illustrative numbers (village example) labeled illustrative in FACTCHECK.
_variant_todo: rewrite every beat narration_text in the Pragmatist register (voices/pragmatist/VOICE.md + HAI.md) — voice only, facts unchanged
signature tangent 0-1 per video, ONLY on a clear opportunity (see SKILL)
swap the outro to the HAI outro from AUTHOR.MD :: Humanitarians AI
then build audience-namespaced (audio in the new voice, scenes in the new palette)

beats.title: Title card
beats.narration_text: H. pylori causes gastric cancer — not by encoding an oncogene, but through decades of persistent colonization driving chronic inflammation.

viz.headline: Why Ulcer Bacteria Also Cause Cancer

build.suggested: suggested prompt: H. pylori causes gastric cancer — not by encoding an oncogene, but through decades of persistent colonization driving chronic inflammation.

beats.title: Stomach colonized — silent, indefinite

props.headline: Half the world carries H. pylori in the stomach lining. Most…

beats.narration_text: Half the world carries H. pylori in the stomach lining. Most remain asymptomatic indefinitely. A subset, decades later, develop gastric adenocarcinoma.

viz.description: Microscopy-style cross-section of stomach mucosa with spiral bacteria colonizing the epithelial surface. Newsprint treatment, cool clinical palette.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: Half the world carries H. pylori in the stomach lining. Most remain asymptomatic indefinitely. A subset, decades later, develop gastric adenocarcinoma.

beats.title: The question — curable bacteria, fatal cancer
beats.narration_text: H. pylori is curable with one week of antibiotics. Yet it causes gastric cancer after 30 to 50 years of colonization — and most infected individuals never develop cancer. The question: why does a curable bacterial infection produce cancer in some people after decades?

viz.question: Why does a curable bacterial infection cause cancer after 30-50 years?

build.suggested: suggested prompt: H. pylori is curable with one week of antibiotics. Yet it causes gastric cancer after 30 to 50 years of colonization — and most infected individuals never devel

beats.title: The naive expectation — search for an oncogene
beats.narration_text: The direct-oncogene model predicts a bacterial protein that hijacks cell growth controls. H. pylori does inject CagA into epithelial cells. But CagA is not a classical transforming protein — it does not drive malignant transformation directly. That model fails.

viz.description: Two-column layout: left column (TEAL, labeled DIRECT ONCOGENE) shows a pathogen injecting a protein arrow straight into a cancer-driver node — clean, one-step. Right column (CRIMSON, labeled H. PYLORI) shows the same expectation crossed out — CagA arrives but no cancer driver lights up.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: The direct-oncogene model predicts a bacterial protein that hijacks cell growth controls. H. pylori does inject CagA into epithelial cells. But CagA is not a cl

beats.title: Why most infected people never get cancer
beats.narration_text: A direct oncogene would predict cancer in most carriers. The actual rate is much lower. Any valid mechanism must account for both the cancer risk and its rarity among carriers.

viz.description: A grid of 100 dots representing H. pylori carriers. The vast majority are TEAL (no cancer). A small cluster at the corner is CRIMSON (cancer). Label: 'most carriers: no cancer'.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: A direct oncogene would predict cancer in most carriers. The actual rate is much lower. Any valid mechanism must account for both the cancer risk and its rarity

beats.title: Section card — the real mechanism
beats.narration_text: The actual mechanism operates at the level of chronic immune activation — not direct transformation.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: The actual mechanism operates at the level of chronic immune activation — not direct transformation.

beats.title: Persistent colonization — the immune engine runs
beats.narration_text: H. pylori colonizes and persists. The immune response — neutrophils and macrophages — generates reactive oxygen and nitrogen species that continuously damage gastric epithelial DNA. Not once. Continuously. For decades.

viz.description: Central bacterium node (CRIMSON) with continuous arrows flowing to immune cells, then ROS/RNS arrows striking a DNA strand. A looping feedback arrow shows the cycle is ongoing — no endpoint. CRIMSON throughout.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: H. pylori colonizes and persists. The immune response — neutrophils and macrophages — generates reactive oxygen and nitrogen species that continuously damage ga

beats.title: Compensatory regeneration — elevated cell division
beats.narration_text: Damaged cells require replacement. Compensatory regeneration drives elevated cell division. More divisions mean more replication events, more replication errors, more accumulated mutations.

viz.description: A horizontal bar representing the epithelium. Small CRIMSON marks (mutations) accumulate along it with each beat. Below: a cell-division counter ticking upward. The bar fills left to right.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: Damaged cells require replacement. Compensatory regeneration drives elevated cell division. More divisions mean more replication events, more replication errors

beats.title: The Correa cascade — geological strata
beats.narration_text: Over 30 to 50 years the stomach advances through a fixed sequence: active gastritis, atrophic gastritis, intestinal metaplasia, dysplasia, adenocarcinoma. Each stage reflects accumulated inflammatory pressure — not a single precipitating event.

viz.description: A horizontal timeline spanning five decades. Five stages appear left to right as geological strata, each a labeled stratum: Gastritis (CRIMSON light), Atrophy, Metaplasia, Dysplasia, Adenocarcinoma (deep CRIMSON). A bacterium icon sits at the far left as the low-grade engine. The strata accumulate one at a time.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: Over 30 to 50 years the stomach advances through a fixed sequence: active gastritis, atrophic gastritis, intestinal metaplasia, dysplasia, adenocarcinoma. Each

beats.title: Eradication interrupts the engine
beats.narration_text: Eradicating H. pylori with antibiotics stops the inflammatory engine. Long-term data: eradication reduces gastric cancer incidence by roughly 35 percent — not because a cancer gene was removed, but because the source of mutagenic pressure was eliminated.

viz.description: Two horizontal arms side by side. Left (TEAL): eradication arrow cuts the engine at year 1, cascade halts at Gastritis, endpoint shows low cancer risk. Right (CRIMSON): no eradication, cascade progresses through all five stages to Adenocarcinoma. Label the 35% reduction on the left arm.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: Eradicating H. pylori with antibiotics stops the inflammatory engine. Long-term data: eradication reduces gastric cancer incidence by roughly 35 percent — not b

beats.title: Why only some — cofactors and latency
beats.narration_text: Most carriers never develop cancer because the mechanism is probabilistic — cumulative mutagenic pressure, not guaranteed transformation. Host genetics, bacterial strain virulence, and timing all modulate who crosses the threshold.

viz.description: A single horizontal line representing the mutation threshold for cancer. Two populations of dots (TEAL = no cancer, CRIMSON = cancer). Dots spread around the line — most fall below, a few cross. No single deterministic arrow — the crossing is probabilistic.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: Most carriers never develop cancer because the mechanism is probabilistic — cumulative mutagenic pressure, not guaranteed transformation. Host genetics, bacteri

beats.title: Two villages — one eradicates, one does not
beats.narration_text: Two villages in rural Japan, high H. pylori prevalence in both. Village A runs an eradication program in the 1990s. Village B does not. By 2020, gastric cancer incidence in Village A is 40 percent lower than Village B. Same genetics, same diet — the only difference is 30 years of inflammatory engine exposure. Illustrative.

viz.description: Two side-by-side bar charts. Left (TEAL): Village A — bar starts high in 1990 (pre-eradication), then drops steeply at 2000, low by 2020. Right (CRIMSON): Village B — bar stays elevated all the way to 2020. Label: 40% lower on the left. Both bars show the same starting prevalence. Illustrative.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: Two villages in rural Japan, high H. pylori prevalence in both. Village A runs an eradication program in the 1990s. Village B does not. By 2020, gastric cancer

beats.title: Endcard
beats.narration_text: H. pylori causes cancer not by carrying an oncogene but by persisting long enough to sustain a decades-long inflammatory engine that assembles driver mutations through cumulative DNA damage. Eliminate the bacterium, and you eliminate the mutagenic pressure.

viz.question: Why does a curable bacterial infection cause cancer after 30-50 years?
viz.answer: Persistent colonization sustains a chronic immune response that generates DNA-damaging reactive species for decades — the inflammation, not the bacterium, assembles the driver mutations.

build.suggested: suggested prompt: H. pylori causes cancer not by carrying an oncogene but by persisting long enough to sustain a decades-long inflammatory engine that assembles driver mutations
